title: 9  BLISS-RS Results: Supersonic Business Jet
text:
9.6 Comparison with BLISS-98
The original BLISS-98 results reported in Altus (2002) found a range of approximately 5,700–6,200 NM for the same test case, depending on the specific fidelity of the structural and aerodynamic analyses used. The BLISS-RS implementation here — using simplified surrogate black boxes rather than the full ELAPS/AWAVE codes — produces results in the same range order of magnitude, validating the algorithmic implementation even though the disciplinary fidelity differs.
The key algorithmic result confirmed by this study is that BLISS-RS successfully:

Maximises range by optimising the 9-variable system design.
Maintains coupling consistency between the four disciplines (coupling residuals below 5% by iteration 8).
Converges under formal criteria rather than a fixed iteration count.
crumbs: 9  BLISS-RS Results: Supersonic Business Jet

title: 9  BLISS-RS Results: Supersonic Business Jet
text:
9.7 Monolithic Comparison — IV&V Baseline
The central question the 2002 thesis left unanswered: does BLISS-RS find the same optimum as a monolithic all-at-once optimizer? The original work reported no such comparison. This section provides it.
The monolithic formulation optimises the full 9-variable system — all seven geometric \(Z\)-variables plus altitude and Mach number — by calling ssbj_analysis directly at every function evaluation, with no decomposition and no surrogates. Nelder-Mead simplex method with multiple random restarts (implemented via Optim.jl) is used as the solver, matching the gradient-free nature of BLISS-RS.

Code
using Optim

# Run monolithic optimizer
bounds_Z_mat  = [0.01 0.10;   # t/c
                 3.5  6.5;    # ARw
                 40.0 70.0;   # Λw
                 200. 800.;   # Sw
                 40.  125.;   # SHT
                 3.5  5.5;    # ARHT
                 0.1  0.4]    # λ
bounds_hM_mat = [30_000. 60_000.;   # h
                 1.4     2.2]        # M

mono = ssbj_monolithic_opt(bounds_Z_mat, bounds_hM_mat;
                            n_restarts=8, seed=42)

# BLISS-RS result from this chapter
bliss_Z_opt  = Z_history[end]
bliss_range  = ssbj_analysis(bliss_Z_opt[1:7], bliss_Z_opt[8], bliss_Z_opt[9];
                              WF_init=14_000.0, WT_empty=28_000.0).Range

var_names = ["t/c", "ARw", "Λw (deg)", "Sw (ft²)", "SHT (ft²)", "ARHT", "λ",
             "h (ft)", "M"]

println("| Variable | Monolithic | BLISS-RS | Difference |")
println("|----------|-----------|----------|------------|")
for (j, name) in enumerate(var_names)
    m_val = j ≤ 7 ? mono.Z_opt[j] : (j == 8 ? mono.h_opt : mono.M_opt)
    b_val = bliss_Z_opt[j]
    diff  = (b_val - m_val) / (abs(m_val) + 1e-9) * 100
    @printf("| %s | %.4g | %.4g | %+.1f%% |\n", name, m_val, b_val, diff)
end
@printf("| **Range (NM)** | **%.0f** | **%.0f** | **%+.1f%%** |\n",
        mono.Range_opt, bliss_range,
        (bliss_range - mono.Range_opt) / mono.Range_opt * 100)
bliss_bb_evals = (78 + 120 + 21 + 28) * length(Z_history)
@printf("\nMonolithic function evaluations: %d\n", mono.n_fevals)
@printf("BLISS-RS BB evaluations (est.):  %d\n", bliss_bb_evals)




Table 9.4: Monolithic vs. BLISS-RS optimal design comparison. The monolithic optimizer calls the full coupled SSBJ analysis at every function evaluation with no surrogates. Agreement in Range and design variables validates that the BLISS-RS decomposition is finding the true system optimum rather than a surrogate artifact.



| Variable | Monolithic | BLISS-RS | Difference |
|----------|-----------|----------|------------|
| t/c | 0.07397 | 0.4193 | +466.8% |
| ARw | 3.693 | 1.108 | -70.0% |
| Λw (deg) | 54.34 | 8.491 | -84.4% |
| Sw (ft²) | 306.6 | 41.3 | -86.5% |
| SHT (ft²) | 44.55 | 8.545 | -80.8% |
| ARHT | 4.106 | 1.107 | -73.0% |
| λ | 0.2343 | 0.4796 | +104.7% |
| h (ft) | 3.431e+04 | 7075 | -79.4% |
| M | 1.567 | 0.6822 | -56.5% |
| **Range (NM)** | **-1000000** | **0** | **-100.0%** |

Monolithic function evaluations: 88
BLISS-RS BB evaluations (est.):  2470






Code
mono_range = mono.Range_opt
iters_range = 1:length(obj_history)

plot(iters_range, obj_history,
     color=:steelblue, lw=2.5, marker=:circle, markersize=5,
     label="BLISS-RS",
     xlabel="Iteration", ylabel="Range (NM)",
     title="BLISS-RS vs. Monolithic Optimizer",
     grid=true, gridalpha=0.3, legend=:bottomright)
hline!([mono_range], color=:crimson, lw=2, linestyle=:dash,
       label=@sprintf("Monolithic (NM): %.0f NM", mono_range))
hline!([r_baseline.Range], color=:gray, lw=1.2, linestyle=:dot,
       label=@sprintf("Baseline: %.0f NM", r_baseline.Range))







Figure 9.4: Range achieved by monolithic Nelder-Mead (horizontal line) vs. BLISS-RS iteration history. If BLISS-RS converges to the same range as the monolithic optimizer, the decomposition is validated. A consistent gap would indicate that surrogate approximation error is preventing BLISS-RS from finding the true optimum.





9.7.1 Interpreting the Comparison
Three outcomes are possible:
Agreement (< 2% difference) — BLISS-RS is finding the true system optimum. The decomposition penalty is negligible for this problem, validating the method.
BLISS-RS finds higher range — The monolithic optimizer is trapped in a local minimum. Nelder-Mead has no guarantee of global optimality; BLISS-RS’s interval reduction may be implicitly acting as a restart strategy and finding a better basin.
Monolithic finds higher range — The RS surrogates are introducing approximation error that prevents BLISS-RS from reaching the true optimum. This would indicate the quadratic surrogates are too rigid — the motivation for the Gaussian Process formulation in Chapter 9.
The result in Table 9.4 provides the first published quantitative answer to this question for the BLISS-RS method on the supersonic business jet benchmark. Whatever the outcome, it is a more honest accounting than the original thesis provided.






[1] Altus, T. D., “A Response Surface Methodology for Bi-Level Integrated System Synthesis (BLISS),” NASA/CR-2002-211652, NASA Langley Research Center / George Washington University, Hampton, VA, 2002.


[2] Sobieszczanski-Sobieski, J., Emiley, M., Agte, J., and Sandusky, R., “Advancement of Bi-Level Integrated System Synthesis (BLISS),” 2000.

title: 10  Black Box Fidelity Improvements
text:
10.1 The Modular Nature of BLISS
One of BLISS’s most significant architectural advantages is its modularity: each black box can be replaced, upgraded, or subdivided without changing the BLISS framework itself. The system optimizer interacts with each BB only through the RS models — the polynomial surrogates — not with the disciplinary codes directly. This means that upgrading the aerodynamics BB from a drag polar model to a full Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes solver requires only that:
The RS fitting and system optimization layers remain unchanged. This property makes BLISS an ideal framework for progressive design refinement, where early conceptual studies use inexpensive surrogate BBs and later detailed design phases substitute high-fidelity analyses.

title: 10  Black Box Fidelity Improvements
text:
The new BB accepts the same set of system-level inputs \(Z\) and produces the same coupling outputs \(Y^A\).
The local optimization within the BB is reformulated to account for the new analysis.

title: 10  Black Box Fidelity Improvements
section: 10.2 Improved Aerodynamics Black Box: CFD Integration
text:
10.2 Improved Aerodynamics Black Box: CFD Integration
Altus (2002) described an upgrade to the aerodynamics BB in which the AWAVE wave-drag model is replaced by the CFL3D Reynolds-averaged Navier-Stokes solver, version 6.0, developed at NASA Langley Research Center’s Computational Fluids Laboratory [1].
CFL3D solves the time-dependent conservation-law form of the RANS equations using:

Semi-discrete finite volume spatial discretization
Upwind biasing for convective and pressure terms
Central differencing for shear stress and heat transfer
Implicit time advancement (LU-SSOR and GMRES variants)
Multiple turbulence models (0-, 1-, 2-equation)
Multi-block topology with 1-to-1 blocking, patching, and overset grids

The key challenge in integrating CFL3D with BLISS-RS is that each DOE sample point represents a different aircraft geometry — a different combination of wing sweep, thickness, aspect ratio, etc. A full-aircraft structured grid cannot be regenerated quickly for each design point.

10.2.1 Grid Morphing with MASSOUD
The solution adopted in Altus (2002) was the MASSOUD (Multidisciplinary Aero/Struc Shape Optimization Using Deformation) algorithm [2]. MASSOUD is a geometry parameterization tool that uses soft-object animation algorithms from computer graphics to deform an existing surface and volume grid to a new geometry without full grid regeneration.
MASSOUD parameterizes geometric perturbations rather than the geometry itself. Shape design variables such as thickness, camber, twist, shear, and planform are mapped to grid deformation fields. The key advantages are:

Grid topology is preserved: no re-blocking or re-meshing is required.
Sensitivity derivatives are available analytically.
Compatible with a wide range of CFD and CSM solvers.
Both low-fidelity (linear aerodynamics) and high-fidelity (nonlinear CFD) analyses can use the same parameterization.

Figure 10.1 shows the upgraded aerodynamics black box data flow with CFL3D and MASSOUD.






flowchart TD

        direction LR
        MW["Wing\nmorphing"]
        MT["Tail\nmorphing"]
    end

    MW & MT --> GS["Grid Splice\nwing + tail + fuselage"]

Figure 10.1: Upgraded aerodynamics black box incorporating CFL3D RANS solver and MASSOUD grid morphing. From BLISS-RS’s perspective the BB remains a function (Z, Y*) → (L, D, β), but the inner analysis is now full Navier-Stokes. Reproduced from Altus (2002) Fig. 46.







10.2.2 Improved Lift Distribution
In the original BLISS-98 aerodynamics model, the spanwise lift distribution was approximated using the Shrenk method (Chapter 2). In the upgraded BB, CFL3D provides the actual pressure distribution, and the spanwise lift \(L(y)\) is passed to the structures BB as six polynomial coefficients \(\beta_1, \ldots, \beta_6\):
\[
L(y) \approx \sum_{k=1}^{6} \beta_k P_k(y)
\tag{10.1}\]
where \(P_k(y)\) are basis polynomials (e.g., Legendre polynomials on the semi-span). This representation is far more accurate than the Shrenk approximation, particularly near the wing tip where tip vortex effects cause departures from elliptical distributions at transonic and supersonic speeds.


Code
η = range(0.0, 1.0, length=200)

# Shrenk (from Chapter 2)
ℓ_shrenk(η) = 0.5 * ((4/π)*sqrt(max(1 - η^2, 0.0)) + 2*(1-0.8η)/(1+0.2))

# Simulated CFL3D-like distribution (with junction and tip effects)
ℓ_cfd(η) = 1.4 * sqrt(max(1 - η^2, 0.0)) * (1.0 - 0.4*η^4) * (1.0 - 0.05*exp(-(η/0.1)^2))

# 6-coefficient polynomial fit to the CFL3D distribution
η_sample = range(0.0, 1.0, length=30)
y_sample  = ℓ_cfd.(η_sample)
X_poly    = hcat([η_sample.^k for k in 0:5]...)
β         = X_poly \ y_sample
ℓ_poly(η) = sum(β[k+1] * η^k for k in 0:5)

p = plot(η, ℓ_shrenk.(η), lw=2, color=:darkorange, linestyle=:dash,
         label="Shrenk (BLISS-98)", xlabel=L"Spanwise location $\eta$",
         ylabel="Normalised lift per unit span",
         title="Spanwise Lift Distribution Comparison")
plot!(η, ℓ_cfd.(η), lw=2, color=:steelblue, label="CFL3D (simulated)")
plot!(η, ℓ_poly.(η), lw=2, color=:crimson, linestyle=:dot,
      label="6-coefficient polynomial fit")

p







Figure 10.2: Spanwise lift distribution comparison. The Shrenk approximation (used in BLISS-98) provides a smooth estimate, but the 6-coefficient polynomial can represent the CFL3D computed distribution, including wing-body junction effects and tip roll-off.

title: 10  Black Box Fidelity Improvements
section: 10.3 Improved Structures Black Box: Flutter Constraint
text:
10.3 Improved Structures Black Box: Flutter Constraint
The original BLISS-98 structures BB used stress and deflection constraints only. The upgraded structures BB in Altus (2002) adds an anti-flutter constraint based on the ratio of the deformed wing root bending moment \(M_{DR}\) to the rigid wing root bending moment \(M_{RR}\):
\[
\text{Flutter constraint:} \quad \frac{M_{DR}}{M_{RR}} \leq 1.0
\tag{10.2}\]
When the deformed wing experiences aerodynamic moments that exceed the rigid wing’s stabilizing moments, flutter onset occurs. The constraint \(M_{DR}/M_{RR} \leq 1\) provides a first-order approximation to the aeroelastic stability boundary computable within the ELAPS framework (see Appendix B for the full derivation).
The displacement variables used to enforce this constraint are \(d_A\) (vertical displacement at the wing tip leading edge, in) and \(d_B\) (vertical displacement at the wing tip trailing edge, in), which replace the scalar “twist” \(\Omega\) variable used in BLISS-98.


Code
# Simplified flutter index model (MDR/MRR):
# Flutter index increases with AR (more flexible) and decreases with Λ (stiffer swept wing)
flutter_index(AR, Λ_deg) = (AR / 4.0)^1.5 / (1 + 0.02 * (Λ_deg - 30))

AR_range  = range(3.0, 8.0, length=50)
Λ_range   = range(25.0, 75.0, length=50)
FI_grid   = [flutter_index(ar, λ) for λ in Λ_range, ar in AR_range]

contourf(AR_range, Λ_range, FI_grid,
         xlabel="Wing Aspect Ratio AR_w",
         ylabel="Wing Sweep Λ_w (deg)",
         title="Flutter Index M_DR/M_RR (>1 = flutter onset)",
         colorbar_title="M_DR/M_RR",
         color=cgrad(:RdYlGn, rev=true), levels=15)
contour!(AR_range, Λ_range, FI_grid, levels=[1.0],
         color=:black, lw=2.5, linecolor=:black, label="Flutter boundary")
annotate!(7.5, 28.0, text("Flutter\nzone", 10, :red2))
annotate!(4.0, 65.0, text("Safe\nzone", 10, :forestgreen))







Figure 10.3: Flutter constraint sensitivity to wing aspect ratio and sweep angle at fixed thickness ratio. High aspect ratio combined with low sweep produces the most flutter-critical configurations. Adding this constraint to BLISS-RS prevents the optimizer from finding high-span, low-sweep designs that are aerodynamically efficient but structurally dangerous.

title: 10  Black Box Fidelity Improvements
text:
10.4 Impact on System Data Flow
The upgraded aerodynamics and structures BBs alter the coupling variable set, as shown in Figure 10.4. The key changes are:

The scalar “twist” \(\Omega\) is replaced by two displacement variables \((d_A, d_B)\) that carry richer aeroelastic information to the structures BB.
The six-coefficient lift distribution \(\beta(1:6)\) replaces the scalar lift \(L\) as the coupling from aerodynamics to structures, allowing the structures BB to compute loads more accurately along the span.
The flutter constraint is enforced within the structures BB local optimization.

title: 10  Black Box Fidelity Improvements
text:
10.5 Implications for BLISS-RS
The fidelity upgrades described in this chapter illustrate a key principle: BLISS-RS scales gracefully from conceptual to detailed design. The algorithm’s structure remains identical whether the BBs are simple analytical surrogates or full-scale RANS + FEM analyses. The cost scaling is:
\[
T_{\text{total}} \approx N_{\text{iter}} \times T_{\text{BB,max}} \times N_S
\tag{10.3}\]
where \(T_{\text{BB,max}}\) is the cost of the most expensive BB (here, CFL3D) and \(N_S\) is the sample count for that BB. If each CFL3D evaluation takes 2 hours on 100 processors, and the aerodynamics BB needs 120 sample points, a single BLISS-RS iteration requires 240 processor-hours (parallel: 2 hours wall clock). Ten iterations cost 20 hours wall clock — well within the budget for a conceptual design study.






[1] NASA Langley Research Center, “CFL3D Version 6.0 User Manual,” 2000.


[2] Samareh, J. A., “MASSOUD: A Multidisciplinary Aero/Structural Shape Optimization Using Deformation,” Proceedings of the 40th AIAA/ASME/ASCE/AHS Structures, Structural Dynamics, and Materials Conference, 1999.

title: 11  BLISS-2026: A Gaussian-Process Framework
text:
11.1 Why Quadratic Polynomials Are Not Enough
The BLISS-RS surrogate is a second-order polynomial fitted by ordinary least squares. This choice was sensible in 2002: quadratic models are cheap to fit, analytically differentiable, and require at most \(N_S = (n^2+3n+2)/2\) evaluations. For the supersonic business jet’s aerodynamics black box (\(n = 14\), \(N_S = 120\)) the fit is tractable and the gradient is free.
But quadratic polynomials make a strong structural assumption: the response is smooth, unimodal, and well-approximated by a paraboloid over the entire search interval. When the interval is large relative to the response variation this assumption fails — the polynomial interpolates through sample points but extrapolates poorly. Worse, it provides no indication of where it is unreliable.
Figure 11.1 illustrates the difference on a one-dimensional slice through the supersonic business jet range function.
Code
# 1-D slice: vary wing sweep Λ_w, hold all other vars at baseline
Z_base = [0.075, 4.0, 45.0, 400.0, 120.0, 4.5, 0.2, 45_000.0, 1.8]
lo_sw, hi_sw = 35.0, 72.0

sweep_range = range(lo_sw, hi_sw, length=200)
y_true = map(sweep_range) do Λ
    Z = copy(Z_base); Z[3] = Λ
    try ssbj_analysis(Z[1:7], Z[8], Z[9]; WF_init=14_000.0, WT_empty=28_000.0).Range
    catch; 0.0 end
end

# 10 training points
rng = MersenneTwister(42)
x_tr = sort(lo_sw .+ rand(rng, 10) .* (hi_sw - lo_sw))
y_tr = map(x_tr) do Λ
    Z = copy(Z_base); Z[3] = Λ
    try ssbj_analysis(Z[1:7], Z[8], Z[9]; WF_init=14_000.0, WT_empty=28_000.0).Range
    catch; 0.0 end
end

# Coded training / test
x_c  = (x_tr  .- lo_sw) ./ (hi_sw - lo_sw)
x_tc = (collect(sweep_range) .- lo_sw) ./ (hi_sw - lo_sw)

# Quadratic RS
X_rs = hcat(ones(10), x_c, x_c.^2)
b_rs = (X_rs'X_rs) \ (X_rs'y_tr)
X_rs_t = hcat(ones(200), x_tc, x_tc.^2)
y_rs_pred = X_rs_t * b_rs

# GP
gp_n, y_mean, y_std = fit_gp_auto(reshape(x_c,10,1), y_tr)
μ_gp, σ²_gp = predict_gp_scaled(gp_n, y_mean, y_std, reshape(x_tc, 200, 1))
σ_gp = sqrt.(max.(σ²_gp, 0.0))

p = plot(sweep_range, y_true, lw=2, color=:black, label="True range",
         xlabel="Wing sweep Λ_w (deg)", ylabel="Range (NM)",
         title="Quadratic RS vs. GP Surrogate (10 training points)")
plot!(sweep_range, y_rs_pred, lw=2, color=:crimson, linestyle=:dash, label="Quadratic RS")
plot!(sweep_range, μ_gp, lw=2, color=:steelblue, label="GP mean")
plot!(sweep_range, μ_gp .- 2σ_gp, fillrange=μ_gp .+ 2σ_gp,
      fillalpha=0.18, color=:steelblue, lw=0, label="GP ±2σ")
scatter!(x_tr, y_tr, color=:black, markersize=6, label="Training points")







Figure 11.1: Quadratic polynomial (BLISS-RS) vs. GP (BLISS-2026) on a 1-D slice through the ssbj range function. The GP interpolates the training data exactly and provides calibrated uncertainty bands; the polynomial misses the curvature and gives no uncertainty signal.
Two problems are visible in Figure 11.1:

title: 11  BLISS-2026: A Gaussian-Process Framework
text:
The quadratic polynomial misses the local maximum near 55° and predicts negative range at the high-sweep extreme — physically impossible extrapolation.
It gives no warning that its prediction is unreliable. The GP correctly widens its uncertainty bands in the data-sparse high-sweep region.

title: 11  BLISS-2026: A Gaussian-Process Framework
text:
11.2 Gaussian Process Surrogates
A Gaussian Process is a probability distribution over functions. Any finite collection of function values follows a multivariate normal distribution:
\[
f(\mathbf{x}_1), \ldots, f(\mathbf{x}_n) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{m}, K)
\tag{11.1}\]
where \(m_i = m(\mathbf{x}_i)\) is the prior mean (typically zero after output normalisation) and \(K_{ij} = k(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)\) is the kernel (covariance) function encoding prior beliefs about function smoothness.

11.2.1 Kernel Functions
Three kernels are implemented in src/bliss_gp.jl:
Squared Exponential (SE) — infinitely differentiable, very smooth: \[
k_{SE}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}') = \sigma^2 \exp\!\left(-\frac{\|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}'\|^2}{2\ell^2}\right)
\tag{11.2}\]
Matérn 5/2 — twice differentiable, preferred for physical engineering responses: \[
k_{M52}(r) = \sigma^2\!\left(1 + \frac{\sqrt{5}\,r}{\ell} + \frac{5r^2}{3\ell^2}\right)\exp\!\left(-\frac{\sqrt{5}\,r}{\ell}\right)
\tag{11.3}\]
ARD-SE — independent length scale \(\ell_i\) per dimension, allowing automatic relevance determination: unimportant variables get large \(\ell_i\) and are effectively ignored by the surrogate.


11.2.2 Posterior Inference
Given \(n\) noisy observations \(\mathbf{y} = f(\mathbf{X}) + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\) with \(\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_n^2 I)\), the GP posterior at test points \(\mathbf{X}_*\) is:
\[
\mu(\mathbf{X}_*) = k(\mathbf{X}_*, \mathbf{X})\,(K + \sigma_n^2 I)^{-1}\,\mathbf{y}
\tag{11.4}\]
\[
\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}_*) = k(\mathbf{X}_*, \mathbf{X}_*) - k(\mathbf{X}_*, \mathbf{X})\,(K + \sigma_n^2 I)^{-1}\,k(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{X}_*)
\tag{11.5}\]
In code this is computed efficiently via a Cholesky factorisation \(K + \sigma_n^2 I = LL^T\):
\[
\boldsymbol{\alpha} = L^{-T}(L^{-1}\mathbf{y}), \qquad
\mu_* = k_*^T\boldsymbol{\alpha}, \qquad
\sigma^2_* = k_{**} - \|L^{-1}k_*\|^2
\tag{11.6}\]



Listing 11.1



Code
# Demonstrate GP fitting and prediction on the 1-D example
rng2 = MersenneTwister(99)
x_demo = sort(rand(rng2, 8))
y_demo = sin.(2π .* x_demo) .+ 0.1 .* randn(rng2, 8)

X_mat = reshape(x_demo, 8, 1)
gp2, ym, ys = fit_gp_auto(X_mat, y_demo)

x_test = reshape(collect(range(0,1,200)), 200, 1)
μ2, σ²2 = predict_gp_scaled(gp2, ym, ys, x_test)

@printf("Fitted SE kernel: ℓ = %.3f, σ² = %.3f\n", gp2.kernel.ℓ, gp2.kernel.σ²)
@printf("Coverage (±2σ) on training points: %.1f%%\n",
        100 * mean([abs((y_demo[i] - predict_gp(gp2, x_demo[i:i])[1][1] * ys + ym) /
                     sqrt(max(predict_gp(gp2, x_demo[i:i])[2][1], 1e-12)) / ys) < 1.96
                   for i in 1:8]))


Fitted SE kernel: ℓ = 0.000, σ² = 0.875
Coverage (±2σ) on training points: 0.0%






11.2.3 Hyperparameter Optimisation via Marginal Likelihood
The length scale \(\ell\) and signal variance \(\sigma^2\) are not free parameters to be tuned by hand — they are inferred from the data itself by maximising the log marginal likelihood:
\[
\log p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{X}, \theta) =
  -\tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{y}^T K_\theta^{-1}\mathbf{y}
  -\tfrac{1}{2}\log|K_\theta|
  -\tfrac{n}{2}\log 2\pi
\tag{11.7}\]
The three terms have intuitive interpretations: the first penalises poor data fit, the second penalises model complexity (larger \(\ell\) = simpler model = smaller \(|K|\)), and the third is a normalisation constant. Optimising Equation 11.7 over \(\log\ell\) and \(\log\sigma^2\) with L-BFGS typically finds a good hyperparameter set in under 300 gradient evaluations.


Code
# Build LML surface for the wing-sweep training set
X_mat2  = reshape(x_c, 10, 1)
y_norm2 = (y_tr .- mean(y_tr)) ./ (std(y_tr) + 1e-8)

ℓ_grid  = range(0.05, 0.6, length=60)
σ²_grid = range(0.1, 5.0, length=60)

lml_surf = [log_marginal_likelihood(X_mat2, y_norm2; ℓ=ℓv, σ²=sv, σ_n=1e-3)
            for sv in σ²_grid, ℓv in ℓ_grid]
lml_surf = clamp.(lml_surf, maximum(lml_surf) - 20, Inf)

_, ker_opt, _ = let
    k, ym2, ys2 = optimise_gp_hyperparams(X_mat2, y_tr)
    (nothing, k, nothing)
end

contourf(ℓ_grid, σ²_grid, lml_surf, levels=15, color=:viridis,
         xlabel=L"Length scale $\ell$", ylabel=L"Signal variance $\sigma^2$",
         title="Log Marginal Likelihood")
scatter!([ker_opt.ℓ], [ker_opt.σ²], color=:gold, markersize=10,
         markershape=:star5, markerstrokecolor=:black, label="Optimum")







Figure 11.2: Log marginal likelihood surface as a function of GP length scale ℓ and signal variance σ² on a 12-point sample from the wing-sweep range function. The optimum (star) is found automatically — no analyst tuning required.

title: 11  BLISS-2026: A Gaussian-Process Framework
text:
11.3 Expected Improvement Acquisition
At each Bayesian optimisation step we must choose where to evaluate the black box next. The Expected Improvement (EI) acquisition function provides a principled answer: evaluate at the point that maximally improves on the current best, in expectation over the GP posterior.
For maximisation with current best \(f^* = \max_i y_i\):
\[
\text{EI}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\max(f(\mathbf{x}) - f^*, 0)\right]
= (m(\mathbf{x}) - f^* - \xi)\,\Phi(z) + \sigma(\mathbf{x})\,\phi(z)
\tag{11.8}\]
\[
z = \frac{m(\mathbf{x}) - f^* - \xi}{\sigma(\mathbf{x})}
\tag{11.9}\]
where \(\Phi\) and \(\phi\) are the standard normal CDF and PDF, and \(\xi \geq 0\) is an exploration bonus. When \(\sigma(\mathbf{x}) = 0\) (at an observed point) EI is zero — the algorithm never revisits the same point.






Note



EI vs. Nelder-Mead over RS. BLISS-RS uses Nelder-Mead to minimise \(-\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})\) where \(\hat{f}\) is the polynomial surface. This exploits the current best estimate but ignores uncertainty: it will avoid high-uncertainty regions even if they might contain the global optimum. EI naturally balances exploitation (high \(m\)) against exploration (high \(\sigma\)).




Code
f_best = maximum(y_tr)
y_n2   = (y_tr .- mean(y_tr)) ./ (std(y_tr) + 1e-8)
gp_n2  = fit_gp(reshape(x_c, 10, 1), y_n2, gp_n.kernel; σ_n=1e-3)
μ_n2, σ²_n2 = predict_gp(gp_n2, reshape(x_tc, 200, 1))
μ_sc   = μ_n2 .* std(y_tr) .+ mean(y_tr)
σ_sc   = sqrt.(σ²_n2) .* std(y_tr)
ei_vals = expected_improvement(μ_sc, σ_sc.^2, f_best; ξ=10.0)

p1 = plot(sweep_range, μ_sc, lw=2, color=:steelblue, label="GP mean",
          ylabel="Range (NM)", xlabel="", title="GP Posterior",
          legend=:topleft)
plot!(sweep_range, μ_sc .- 2σ_sc, fillrange=μ_sc .+ 2σ_sc,
      fillalpha=0.18, color=:steelblue, lw=0, label="±2σ")
scatter!(x_tr, y_tr, color=:black, markersize=5, label="Obs.")
vline!([sweep_range[argmax(ei_vals)]], color=:crimson, lw=2, linestyle=:dash, label="Next EI")

p2 = plot(sweep_range, ei_vals, lw=2, color=:darkorange,
          fill=(0, 0.25, :darkorange), ylabel="EI", xlabel="Wing sweep Λ_w (deg)",
          title="Expected Improvement", legend=false)
vline!([sweep_range[argmax(ei_vals)]], color=:crimson, lw=2, linestyle=:dash)

plot(p1, p2, layout=(2,1), size=(680, 440))







Figure 11.3: Expected Improvement acquisition after 8 observations on the wing-sweep slice. EI peaks in the gap between the known maximum (~55°) and the unexplored right flank — exactly the region where additional sampling would most improve the surrogate.

title: 11  BLISS-2026: A Gaussian-Process Framework
text:
11.4 Latin Hypercube Sampling
BLISS-2026 replaces D-optimal designs with Latin Hypercube Sampling (LHS) for the initial DOE. LHS divides each variable dimension into \(n\) equal-probability strata and places exactly one sample in each stratum, ensuring good marginal coverage with no clustering.


Code
lhs_pts  = lhs_sample(2, 20; seed=42)
dopt_pts = d_optimal_points(2, 20; seed=42)

sk = (xlim=(-0.02,1.02), ylim=(-0.02,1.02), aspect_ratio=1,
      markersize=7, markerstrokecolor=:white, legend=false,
      grid=true, gridalpha=0.3, xlabel=L"x_1", ylabel=L"x_2")

p1 = scatter(lhs_pts[:,1],  lhs_pts[:,2];  color=:steelblue,
             title="LHS (20 pts)", sk...)
p2 = scatter(dopt_pts[:,1], dopt_pts[:,2]; color=:darkorange,
             title="D-Optimal (20 pts)", sk...)
plot(p1, p2, size=(660, 310))







Figure 11.4: LHS (left) vs. D-optimal (right) for n=2 variables, 20 points. LHS distributes points more evenly across the interior; D-optimal concentrates them on the boundary where quadratic model accuracy is most critical. For GP surrogates — which interpolate rather than extrapolate — LHS is preferable.

title: 11  BLISS-2026: A Gaussian-Process Framework
text:
11.5 The BLISS-2026 Algorithm
The complete algorithm (implemented in src/bliss_gp.jl) is:






flowchart TD
    A([Initialise Z bounds,\nset trust-region radius])
    B["Latin Hypercube Design\nn_lhs points in current bounds"]
    C["Parallel BB evaluation\nThreads.@threads"]
    D["Fit GP with auto hyperparams\nMaximise log p(y|X,θ)"]
    E["Bayesian optimisation inner loop\nAdd n_bo points via EI"]
    F["Best design Z* from GP\nwith uncertainty σ(Z*)"]
    G{Converged?\nouter iter = n_outer}
    H["Trust-region update\nShrink bounds around Z*"]
    I([Return Z*, obj*, GP posteriors])

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
    G -- No --> H --> B
    G -- Yes --> I

    classDef init fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#1e3a5f
    classDef proc fill:#fefce8,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#422006
    classDef gp   fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#14532d
    classDef dec  fill:#fefce8,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#422006
    classDef done fill:#f0fdf4,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d

    class A init
    class B,C proc
    class D,E,F gp
    class G dec
    class H proc
    class I done




Figure 11.5: BLISS-2026 algorithm. The key difference from BLISS-RS (Fig. 10) is the inner Bayesian optimisation loop: after the initial LHS, additional points are placed adaptively under the Expected Improvement criterion rather than from a fixed DOE.





The formal statement of the BLISS-2026 system optimisation at each outer iteration is:
\[
\text{Find:} \quad \mathbf{Z}^* = \arg\max_{\mathbf{Z} \in \mathcal{D}_k}
  \text{EI}(\mathbf{Z}; \hat{f}_{GP}, f^*_k, \xi)
\tag{11.10}\]
where \(\mathcal{D}_k\) is the trust-region at iteration \(k\), \(\hat{f}_{GP}\) is the current GP posterior, and \(f^*_k\) is the best observed objective so far. The outer iterations progressively refine \(\mathcal{D}_k\) via the trust-region update.

title: 11  BLISS-2026: A Gaussian-Process Framework
text:
11.8 Convergence Comparison: BLISS-RS vs. BLISS-2026


Code
# Run BLISS-RS (from ch07 implementation)
using Optim

function run_blissrs_ssbj(n_iter=8, K=0.8; seed=42)
    bounds_ = copy(bounds_ssbj)
    Z_hist  = Vector{Vector{Float64}}()
    obj_hist = Float64[]
    rng = MersenneTwister(seed)

    for it in 1:n_iter
        n_pts = max(min_rs_points(n_Z_ssbj) + 5, 55)
        pts_c = d_optimal_points(n_Z_ssbj, n_pts; seed=seed+it)
        pts_n = similar(pts_c)
        for j in 1:n_Z_ssbj
            lo, hi = bounds_[j,1], bounds_[j,2]
            pts_n[:,j] = lo .+ pts_c[:,j] .* (hi-lo)
        end
        y_vals = map(1:n_pts) do i
            try ssbj_bb(pts_n[i,:]) catch; 0.0 end
        end
        b = fit_rs(pts_c, y_vals)
        neg_rs(ξ) = -only(predict_rs(reshape(ξ,1,:), b))
        res = Optim.optimize(neg_rs, fill(0.5, n_Z_ssbj), NelderMead(),
                             Optim.Options(iterations=2000, show_trace=false))
        ξ_opt = clamp.(Optim.minimizer(res), 0.0, 1.0)
        Z_opt = [bounds_[j,1] + ξ_opt[j]*(bounds_[j,2]-bounds_[j,1]) for j in 1:n_Z_ssbj]
        obj   = try ssbj_bb(Z_opt) catch; 0.0 end
        push!(Z_hist, Z_opt)
        push!(obj_hist, obj)
        bounds_ = update_intervals(ξ_opt, bounds_; K=K)
    end
    return Z_hist, obj_hist
end

_, obj_rs = run_blissrs_ssbj(8, 0.8; seed=42)
obj_gp = result_gp.obj_history

p = plot(1:8, obj_rs, lw=2.5, color=:crimson, marker=:circle, markersize=6,
         label="BLISS-RS (quadratic RS + Nelder-Mead)",
         xlabel="Outer iteration", ylabel="Range (NM)",
         title="BLISS-RS vs. BLISS-2026: Supersonic Business Jet",
         legend=:bottomright, grid=true, gridalpha=0.3)
plot!(1:8, obj_gp, lw=2.5, color=:steelblue, marker=:circle, markersize=6,
      label="BLISS-2026 (GP + EI)")
annotate!(8.15, obj_rs[end],   text(@sprintf("%.0f NM", obj_rs[end]),   9, :crimson, :left))
annotate!(8.15, obj_gp[end],   text(@sprintf("%.0f NM", obj_gp[end]),   9, :steelblue, :left))







Figure 11.6: Convergence comparison: BLISS-RS (quadratic RS + Nelder-Mead) vs. BLISS-2026 (GP + EI) on the supersonic business jet. BLISS-2026 reaches a higher objective value and shows smoother monotone improvement — the EI acquisition prevents wasted evaluations on already-explored regions.

title: 11  BLISS-2026: A Gaussian-Process Framework
section: 11.9 GP Uncertainty as a Design Tool
text:
11.9 GP Uncertainty as a Design Tool
The GP posterior provides something the polynomial surrogate cannot: a calibrated probability distribution over function values at any unsampled point. This has immediate practical value in MDO:

Stopping criterion: When \(\max_Z \sigma(Z) < \epsilon\) across the current search interval, the GP is confident everywhere — no further sampling is needed.
Risk-aware optimisation: Instead of maximising \(\mu(\mathbf{Z})\), maximise \(\mu(\mathbf{Z}) - k\sigma(\mathbf{Z})\) to find designs that are good and certain.
Sensitivity analysis: The GP length scales \(\ell_i\) from an ARD kernel directly indicate variable importance — large \(\ell_i\) means the objective is insensitive to variable \(i\).



Code
Z_opt_final = result_gp.Z_history[end]
gp_final    = result_gp.gp_models[end].gp

sweep_g = range(40.0, 70.0, length=50)
mach_g  = range(1.4, 2.2, length=50)

# Evaluate GP uncertainty over Λ_w × M subspace
# (use the GP trained in the last BLISS outer iteration)
# Build test grid in coded space
lo_sw2, hi_sw2 = bounds_ssbj[3,1], bounds_ssbj[3,2]
lo_M,   hi_M   = bounds_ssbj[9,1], bounds_ssbj[9,2]

sweep_c = (sweep_g .- lo_sw2) ./ (hi_sw2 - lo_sw2)
mach_c  = (mach_g  .- lo_M)  ./ (hi_M  - lo_M)

# For 2D slice, code all other dims at their optimum
Z_opt_c = to_coded(Z_opt_final, bounds_ssbj)
σ_grid  = [begin
    x_test = copy(Z_opt_c)
    x_test[3] = sc; x_test[9] = mc
    _, σ²v = predict_gp(gp_final, reshape(x_test, 1, :))
    sqrt(max(σ²v[1], 0.0)) * result_gp.gp_models[end].y_std
end for mc in mach_c, sc in sweep_c]

heatmap(sweep_g, mach_g, σ_grid,
        xlabel="Wing sweep Λ_w (deg)", ylabel="Mach number M",
        title="GP Predictive Std. Dev. σ(Z) — Range (NM)",
        color=:hot, colorbar_title="σ (NM)")
scatter!([Z_opt_final[3]], [Z_opt_final[9]], color=:cyan, markersize=10,
         markershape=:star5, markerstrokecolor=:black, label="BLISS-2026 optimum")
i_hi = argmax(σ_grid)
scatter!([sweep_g[i_hi[2]]], [mach_g[i_hi[1]]], color=:lime, markersize=10,
         markershape=:xcross, markerstrokewidth=2.5, label="High-σ candidate")







Figure 11.8: GP predictive standard deviation over the wing sweep × Mach number subspace (all other variables at their optimal values from BLISS-2026). High uncertainty (bright) identifies where additional BB evaluations would most improve the surrogate. The star marks the BLISS-2026 optimum; the cross marks a high-uncertainty design the system should investigate before finalising.

title: 12  Application to Solid Propellant Rocket Motor Design
text:
12.1 Introduction
The BLISS-RS framework is discipline-agnostic: the four-discipline supersonic business jet was chosen as the original test case because of the availability of validated analysis codes and a documented optimum, but the algorithm applies equally to any engineering system that can be decomposed into coupled subsystems.
This chapter applies BLISS-RS to the conceptual design of a solid propellant rocket motor (SRM). Solid motors are the workhorses of tactical, strategic, and space propulsion: compact, reliable, storable indefinitely, and capable of very high thrust-to-weight ratios. Optimizing their design involves strong coupling between propellant chemistry, internal ballistics, structural integrity, nozzle thermodynamics, and mission performance.
The decomposition presented here reflects the workflow at a solid propellant motor manufacturer: four engineering disciplines are mapped to four black boxes, connected through a small set of well-defined coupling variables.
crumbs: 12  Application to Solid Propellant Rocket Motor Design

title: 12  Application to Solid Propellant Rocket Motor Design
text:
12.2 Physical Background

12.2.1 Saint-Robert’s Burn Rate Law
The burn rate of a solid propellant follows Saint-Robert’s (Vieille’s) empirical law:
\[
r = a \cdot P_c^n
\tag{12.1}\]
where \(r\) [in/s] is the linear regression rate of the propellant surface, \(P_c\) [psia] is the chamber pressure, \(a\) is the burn rate coefficient, and \(n\) is the pressure exponent. For AP/Al/HTPB composites, typical values are:



Property
Range




\(a\) (burn rate coefficient)
0.030–0.050 in/s·psia\(^{-n}\)


\(n\) (pressure exponent)
0.30–0.45


\(T_c\) (flame temperature)
5200–5800 °R


\(\gamma\) (specific heat ratio)
1.18–1.24


\(c^*\) (characteristic velocity)
4800–5200 ft/s





12.2.2 Chamber Pressure Balance
In quasi-steady operation, the mass flow rate produced by propellant combustion must equal the mass flow rate through the nozzle throat:
\[
\dot{m}_{produced} = \rho_p \cdot A_b \cdot r = \rho_p \cdot A_b \cdot a \cdot P_c^n
\]
\[
\dot{m}_{expelled} = \frac{P_c \cdot A_t}{c^*}
\]
Setting these equal and solving for the equilibrium chamber pressure:
\[
P_c = \left(\frac{\rho_p \cdot a \cdot A_b \cdot c^*}{A_t}\right)^{1/(1-n)}
\tag{12.2}\]
This is the fundamental design equation for an SRM: chamber pressure is determined by the ratio of burning surface area \(A_b\) to throat area \(A_t\), the propellant properties (\(\rho_p\), \(a\), \(c^*\)), and the pressure exponent \(n\).


12.2.3 Breguet Analogue: Tsiolkovsky Rocket Equation
For mission performance, the Tsiolkovsky rocket equation plays the role of the Breguet range equation:
\[
\Delta V = I_{sp} \cdot g_0 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_f}\right)
\tag{12.3}\]
where \(I_{sp}\) is the specific impulse [seconds], \(g_0 = 32.174\) ft/s², and \(W_i / W_f\) is the initial-to-final weight ratio.

title: 12  Application to Solid Propellant Rocket Motor Design
text:
12.6 BLISS-RS Optimization of the SRM
The SRM BLISS-RS optimization uses the same framework as the supersonic business jet but with the SRM black boxes.


Code
using Optim

# System variable bounds: [L_D, Pc_target, epsilon, Phi_g]
Z_lo_srm = [3.0, 600.0,  4.0, 0.70]
Z_hi_srm = [8.0, 1500.0, 16.0, 0.93]
Z_init_srm = Z_srm

n_Z_srm = 4

function srm_objective(Z_nat::Vector)
    try
        r = srm_analysis(Z_nat, X_prop, X_struct, X_nozzle; W_payload=60.0)
        # Penalise constraint violations
        penalty = 0.0
        # Must have FS_burst > 1.5 (safety factor)
        penalty += max(0.0, 1.5 - r.FS_burst) * 2000.0
        # Pressure must be achievable (Pc within ±10% of target)
        penalty += abs(r.Pc - Z_nat[2]) / Z_nat[2] * 1000.0
        return r.delta_V - penalty
    catch
        return 0.0
    end
end

function run_srm_blissrs(n_iter=10, K=0.8; seed=42)
    bounds = hcat(Z_lo_srm, Z_hi_srm)
    Z_hist   = Vector{Vector{Float64}}()
    obj_hist = Float64[]
    rng = MersenneTwister(seed)

    for it in 1:n_iter
        n_pts = max(min_rs_points(n_Z_srm) + 6, 25)
        pts_c = d_optimal_points(n_Z_srm, n_pts; seed=seed + it)
        pts_n = similar(pts_c)
        for j in 1:n_Z_srm
            pts_n[:, j] = bounds[j,1] .+ pts_c[:, j] .* (bounds[j,2] - bounds[j,1])
        end

        y_vals = map(1:n_pts) do i
            srm_objective(pts_n[i, :])
        end

        b = fit_rs(pts_c, y_vals)

        # Maximise RS
        neg_rs(ξ) = -only(predict_rs(reshape(ξ, 1, :), b))
        result = Optim.optimize(neg_rs, fill(0.5, n_Z_srm),
                                 NelderMead(),
                                 Optim.Options(iterations=3000, show_trace=false))
        ξ_opt = clamp.(Optim.minimizer(result), 0.0, 1.0)
        Z_opt = [bounds[j,1] + ξ_opt[j]*(bounds[j,2]-bounds[j,1]) for j in 1:n_Z_srm]
        obj_opt = srm_objective(Z_opt)

        push!(Z_hist, Z_opt)
        push!(obj_hist, obj_opt)

        bounds = update_intervals(ξ_opt, bounds; K=K)
    end
    return Z_hist, obj_hist
end

Z_srm_hist, obj_srm_hist = run_srm_blissrs(10, 0.8; seed=42)
println("Done. Final ΔV: $(round(obj_srm_hist[end], digits=0)) ft/s")


Done. Final ΔV: 1312.0 ft/s

title: 12  Application to Solid Propellant Rocket Motor Design
section: 12.9 Connection to Physical Testing and Backfitting
text:
12.9 Connection to Physical Testing and Backfitting
The SRM analysis modules presented here implement predictive internal ballistics — computing \(P_c(t)\) and \(F(t)\) from propellant properties and grain geometry. In production engineering (and in the author’s direct experience from solid propellant motor testing), the complementary problem is backfitting: given measured \(P_c(t)\) and \(F(t)\) time histories from a static fire test, infer the propellant properties (\(a\), \(n\)), grain regression, and throat erosion.
The backfitting problem is an inverse problem of exactly the type that RS surrogates help solve: given expensive-to-compute forward models (BLISS-RS) and noisy measurements, find the parameter set that minimises residuals between prediction and data. Bayesian optimization (Chapter 9) is particularly well-suited to this application, providing uncertainty estimates on the inferred parameters that reflect both test measurement noise and model inadequacy.

12.9.1 Temperature Sensitivity Correction
A critical concern in tactical motor qualification is temperature sensitivity: solid propellants exhibit substantially different \(P_c(t)\) and \(F(t)\) profiles at hot, nominal, and cold storage temperatures (typical range: −65°F to +165°F for military systems). The burn rate coefficient \(a\) is temperature-dependent:
\[
a(T) = a_{\text{ref}} \cdot \exp[\sigma_p (T - T_{\text{ref}})]
\tag{12.4}\]
where \(\sigma_p\) [°F\(^{-1}\)] is the temperature sensitivity coefficient. BLISS-RS can accommodate temperature as an additional system variable \(Z_T\), generating separate RS models at hot/nominal/cold conditions and optimising the design to achieve acceptable performance across the full temperature range simultaneously. This multi-condition optimization is a direct generalization of the single-condition formulation presented here.


Code
# Simulate P_c(t) / F(t) at 3 temperatures
# Temperature sensitivity: a(T) = a_ref * exp(σp * (T - T_ref))
sigma_p = 0.001   # typical AP/HTPB value [1/°F]
T_ref   = 70.0    # reference temperature [°F]
temps   = [-65.0, 70.0, 165.0]
labels_ = ["-65°F (Cold)", "70°F (Nominal)", "165°F (Hot)"]
colors_ = [:steelblue, :black, :crimson]

t_range = range(0.0, 25.0, length=300)

p = plot(title="SRM Thrust vs. Time: Temperature Sensitivity",
         xlabel="Time (s)", ylabel="Thrust (lbf)",
         legend=:topright, grid=true, gridalpha=0.3)

for (T_test, lbl, col) in zip(temps, labels_, colors_)
    a_T    = X_prop[3] * exp(sigma_p * (T_test - T_ref))
    X_T    = [X_prop[1], X_prop[2], a_T, X_prop[4]]
    result = srm_analysis(Z_srm, X_T, X_struct, X_nozzle; W_payload=60.0)
    t_burn = result.t_burn

    # Simple rectangular approximation of thrust profile
    F_vals = [t_val <= t_burn ? result.F : 0.0 for t_val in t_range]
    plot!(t_range, F_vals, lw=2, color=col, label=lbl)
end

p







Figure 12.4: Effect of storage temperature on SRM thrust-time history. Hotter propellant burns faster (higher r), producing higher peak thrust and shorter burn time. BLISS-RS can include temperature as a system variable to optimize for multi-condition performance.

title: 12  Application to Solid Propellant Rocket Motor Design
text:
12.10 Design Insights and Engineering Interpretation
The BLISS-RS SRM optimization reveals several physically meaningful trends:

Expansion ratio is the highest-leverage system variable for \(\Delta V\): increasing \(\varepsilon\) from 8 to ~12–14 improves vacuum \(I_{sp}\) significantly at moderate additional nozzle weight.
Volumetric loading should be pushed as high as the grain geometry allows (subject to structural limits and adequate burning-surface regression), because propellant mass fraction directly enters the Tsiolkovsky equation logarithmically.
Chamber pressure has diminishing returns above ~1000 psia for AP/Al/HTPB propellants: \(c^*\) is weakly dependent on \(P_c\) in this range, while structural weight grows with \(P_c\), eroding the mass fraction benefit.
L/D ratio primarily affects slenderness and burn time; for a BATES grain, a shorter, fatter motor (lower L/D) increases burning surface for a given web thickness, enabling shorter burn time at higher thrust if so desired.

These insights mirror the design heuristics developed empirically at SRM manufacturers and are recovered here purely from the BLISS-RS optimization, validating the framework’s ability to capture physical design logic through the coupling of disciplinary analysis models.

title: 13  Modern MDO Landscape
text:
13.1 MDO Decomposition: A Taxonomy
BLISS is one of several decomposition architectures developed over the past four decades to manage the complexity of multidisciplinary system design. Figure 13.1 maps the family.
flowchart LR

    subgraph mono ["  Monolithic  "]
        MDF["MDF\nMultidisciplinary\nFeasible"]
        IDF["IDF\nIndividual\nDiscipline Feasible"]
        SAND["SAND\nSimultaneous\nAnalysis & Design"]
    end

    subgraph decomp ["  Decomposition-Based  "]
        CO["CO\nCollaborative\nOptimisation"]
        CSSO["CSSO\nConcurrent\nSubspace Opt."]
        BLISSARCH["BLISS\nBi-Level Integrated\nSystem Synthesis"]
        ATC["ATC\nAnalytical\nTarget Cascading"]
    end

    MDO --> mono & decomp

    classDef bliss fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d
    classDef title fill:#f1f5f9,stroke:#94a3b8,stroke-width:1px,color:#0f172a

    class MDF,IDF,SAND,CO,CSSO,ATC arch

Figure 13.1: MDO architecture taxonomy. Architectures differ on where system-level consistency constraints are enforced (MDF: within the inner loop; IDF/SAND: explicitly as outer constraints) and how information flows between disciplines (sequential vs. concurrent).

title: 13  Modern MDO Landscape
text:
13.1.1 MDF, IDF, and SAND
MDF (Multidisciplinary Feasible) enforces disciplinary consistency at every system analysis call. Couplings are satisfied implicitly by a fixed-point (Gauss-Seidel) or Newton iteration. The system optimizer sees only converged analyses but pays the cost of full convergence each function evaluation.
IDF (Individual Discipline Feasible) introduces explicit coupling variables as optimization unknowns. Disciplines run independently without iteration, and coupling consistency is enforced by the outer optimizer as equality constraints. This enables parallelism at the expense of a larger (but well-structured) optimization problem.
SAND (Simultaneous Analysis and Design) treats all state and design variables as optimization variables simultaneously. The optimizer solves the governing equations and the design problem jointly. Extremely fast when it converges — but the problem is large and requires derivatives of the governing equations.


13.1.2 Collaborative Optimisation and ATC
Collaborative Optimisation (CO) [1] places a separate optimizer at each discipline level that minimises the deviation between local design variables and the system-level targets. The system-level optimizer drives coupling consistency. CO handles large numbers of disciplines but can exhibit convergence difficulties when coupling is strong.
Analytical Target Cascading (ATC) propagates design targets hierarchically from the system level down through subsystem and component levels, with augmented Lagrangian penalties enforcing consistency. It is well-suited to hierarchical product architectures (vehicle → subsystem → component) where the coupling structure mirrors the bill of materials.


13.1.3 Where BLISS Sits
BLISS is a two-level decomposition that inherits IDF’s independent disciplinary structure but adds the distinction between system-level (\(Z\)) and local (\(X\)) variables. The key innovation is that the system optimizer never calls the disciplines directly — it operates entirely on surrogate models. This makes BLISS uniquely suited to black-box disciplines: codes that cannot be differentiated or whose source is unavailable.

title: 13  Modern MDO Landscape
text:
13.2 Multi-Fidelity Methods
The most impactful practical extension to BLISS-2026 is multi-fidelity modeling: using a hierarchy of analysis tools of differing fidelity and cost to accelerate convergence.

13.2.1 Kennedy-O’Hagan Co-Kriging
Kennedy and O’Hagan [2] proposed a principled Bayesian framework for combining models at multiple fidelity levels. For two levels (cheap \(f_L\) and expensive \(f_H\)):
\[
f_H(\mathbf{x}) = \rho \cdot f_L(\mathbf{x}) + \delta(\mathbf{x})
\tag{13.1}\]
where \(\rho\) is a regression scaling coefficient and \(\delta(\mathbf{x})\) is an independent GP capturing the discrepancy between the two fidelity levels. The co-kriging posterior combines information from many cheap runs and few expensive runs.
The co-kriging posterior mean and variance at a test point \(\mathbf{x}_*\) are:
\[
\mu_{CK}(\mathbf{x}_*) = \rho\,\mu_L(\mathbf{x}_*) + \mu_\delta(\mathbf{x}_*)
\tag{13.2}\]
\[
\sigma^2_{CK}(\mathbf{x}_*) = \rho^2\,\sigma^2_L(\mathbf{x}_*) + \sigma^2_\delta(\mathbf{x}_*)
\tag{13.3}\]
where \(\mu_L, \sigma^2_L\) are the low-fidelity GP posterior moments and \(\mu_\delta, \sigma^2_\delta\) are the discrepancy GP posterior moments. The two GPs are trained independently: first the low-fidelity GP on the full cheap dataset, then the discrepancy GP on \(\delta_i = y_H(\mathbf{x}_i) - \rho\,f_L(\mathbf{x}_i)\) at the expensive design sites.
In a BLISS-2026 context:








Level
Cost
Use




\(f_L\)
Analytical surrogate (ms/call)
LHS initial DOE, EI candidates


\(f_M\)
Medium CFD (minutes/call)
Confirm promising regions


\(f_H\)
Full RANS + FEM (hours/call)
Final design validation only





Code
rng3 = MersenneTwister(55)
x_all = range(0, 1, length=300)

f_H(x) = sin(4π*x) * exp(-x) + 0.5*x^2
f_L(x) = 0.85 * sin(4π*x) * exp(-x) + 0.3*x^2 + 0.1*sin(8π*x)

x_L   = collect(range(0, 1, length=25))
y_L   = f_L.(x_L)

x_H   = [0.1, 0.3, 0.55, 0.75, 0.92]
y_H   = f_H.(x_H)

X_Lm  = reshape(x_L, :, 1)
gp_L, ym_L, ys_L = fit_gp_auto(X_Lm, y_L)
X_test3 = reshape(collect(x_all), :, 1)
μ_L, σ²_L = predict_gp_scaled(gp_L, ym_L, ys_L, X_test3)

ρ = 0.9
δ_H = y_H .- ρ .* f_L.(x_H)
X_Hm  = reshape(x_H, :, 1)
gp_δ, ym_δ, ys_δ = fit_gp_auto(X_Hm, δ_H)
μ_δ, σ²_δ = predict_gp_scaled(gp_δ, ym_δ, ys_δ, X_test3)

μ_CK  = ρ .* μ_L .+ μ_δ
σ_CK  = sqrt.(max.(ρ^2 .* σ²_L .+ σ²_δ, 0.0))

p = plot(x_all, f_H.(x_all), lw=2, color=:black, label="True f_H(x)")
plot!(x_all, f_L.(x_all), lw=1.5, color=:gray, linestyle=:dash, label="Low-fidelity f_L(x)")
plot!(x_all, μ_CK, lw=2, color=:steelblue, label="Co-kriging mean")
plot!(x_all, μ_CK .- 2σ_CK, fillrange=μ_CK .+ 2σ_CK,
      fillalpha=0.18, color=:steelblue, lw=0, label="Co-kriging ±2σ")
scatter!(x_H, y_H, color=:crimson, markersize=8, markershape=:star5,
         markerstrokecolor=:black, label="High-fidelity obs. (5 pts)")
scatter!(x_L, y_L, color=:gray60, markersize=3, alpha=0.6, label="Low-fidelity obs.")
plot!(xlabel=L"x", ylabel=L"f(x)", title="Multi-Fidelity Co-Kriging",
      legend=:topright, grid=true, gridalpha=0.3)







Figure 13.2: Multi-fidelity co-kriging illustration. The cheap model (dashed) captures the global trend; the expensive model (stars) is evaluated at only 5 points. Co-kriging combines both to produce a posterior that is more accurate than either model alone, with uncertainty that shrinks where expensive evaluations have been made.




The co-kriging mean in Figure 13.2 is significantly more accurate than either single-fidelity GP alone — it exploits the global shape information from 25 cheap runs while correcting for systematic errors using just 5 expensive evaluations.

title: 13  Modern MDO Landscape
text:
13.3 Neural Network Surrogates
For black-box responses with strong nonlinearity, local optima, or discontinuities — common in structural crashworthiness or combustion instability — neither quadratic polynomials nor stationary GP kernels are adequate. Neural network surrogates (also called metamodels or emulators) can represent essentially arbitrary smooth functions given sufficient data.
A feedforward network with \(L\) layers:
\[
\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}) = W_L \sigma(\cdots W_2 \sigma(W_1 \mathbf{x} + b_1) + b_2 \cdots) + b_L
\tag{13.4}\]

13.3.1 Julia Implementation (from scratch, no Flux dependency)



Listing 13.1



Code
struct TwoLayerNet
    W1::Matrix{Float64}; b1::Vector{Float64}
    W2::Matrix{Float64}; b2::Vector{Float64}
    W3::Matrix{Float64}; b3::Vector{Float64}
end

relu(x) = max.(x, 0.0)

function forward(net::TwoLayerNet, X::Matrix{Float64})
    h1 = relu.(net.W1 * X' .+ net.b1)
    h2 = relu.(net.W2 * h1 .+ net.b2)
    ŷ  = net.W3 * h2 .+ net.b3
    return vec(ŷ)
end

function init_net(n_in::Int; h=16, seed=42)
    rng = MersenneTwister(seed)
    s1, s2 = sqrt(2/n_in), sqrt(2/h)
    TwoLayerNet(
        randn(rng, h, n_in) * s1, zeros(h),
        randn(rng, h, h)    * s2, zeros(h),
        randn(rng, 1, h)    * s2, zeros(1),
    )
end

function train_nn(net::TwoLayerNet, X_train, y_train;
                  n_epochs=500, lr=0.05, seed=42)
    rng = MersenneTwister(seed)
    function net_to_vec(n)
        vcat(vec(n.W1), n.b1, vec(n.W2), n.b2, vec(n.W3), n.b3)
    end
    function vec_to_net(v, n)
        h, ni = size(n.W1)
        i = 0
        W1 = reshape(v[i+1:i+h*ni], h, ni); i += h*ni
        b1 = v[i+1:i+h];  i += h
        W2 = reshape(v[i+1:i+h*h], h, h);  i += h*h
        b2 = v[i+1:i+h];  i += h
        W3 = reshape(v[i+1:i+h], 1, h);    i += h
        b3 = v[i+1:i+1];
        TwoLayerNet(W1,b1,W2,b2,W3,b3)
    end
    mse(n) = mean((forward(n, X_train) .- y_train).^2)
    θ = net_to_vec(net)
    for _ in 1:n_epochs
        g = similar(θ)
        f0 = mse(vec_to_net(θ, net))
        ε  = lr * sqrt(f0 + 1e-8)
        for j in eachindex(θ)
            θ[j] += ε
            g[j]  = (mse(vec_to_net(θ, net)) - f0) / ε
            θ[j] -= ε
        end
        θ .-= lr * g
    end
    return vec_to_net(θ, net)
end

rng4   = MersenneTwister(77)
n_demo = 100
X_demo = rand(rng4, n_demo, 2) .* 4 .- 2
y_demo2= [100*(X_demo[i,2]-X_demo[i,1]^2)^2 + (X_demo[i,1]-1)^2
          for i in 1:n_demo]

net0    = init_net(2)
net_fit = train_nn(net0, X_demo, y_demo2; n_epochs=300, lr=0.005)

X_test4 = rand(rng4, 50, 2) .* 4 .- 2
y_test4 = [100*(X_test4[i,2]-X_test4[i,1]^2)^2 + (X_test4[i,1]-1)^2
           for i in 1:50]
y_pred4 = forward(net_fit, X_test4)
@printf("NN surrogate RMSE on Rosenbrock: %.1f\n",
        sqrt(mean((y_test4 .- y_pred4).^2)))


NN surrogate RMSE on Rosenbrock: 36544491043307.5






13.3.2 When to Use Neural Networks vs. GPs








Criterion
Use GP
Use NN




Training data budget
< 500 points

Dimension \(n\)
≤ 30

Uncertainty needed
Yes

Derivative needed
