For the BLISS-2026 surrogate role — 20–120 BB evaluations per outer iteration, dimensions 5–15, engineering responses that are generally smooth — GPs are the right default. Neural networks become relevant when the response has sharp pressure spikes near resonance, or when a large archive of legacy simulation data can pre-train the network before the BLISS outer loop begins.

title: 13  Modern MDO Landscape
text:
13.4 Physics-Informed Neural Networks
A key limitation of purely data-driven surrogates — whether GP or NN — is that they ignore the physics. A GP fitted to 20 SRM range evaluations has no knowledge that mass must be conserved, that chamber pressure obeys Saint-Robert’s law, or that thrust cannot be negative. Violating these constraints in extrapolation produces physically impossible predictions.
Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) [3] address this by adding residuals of the governing equations directly to the training loss. For a system governed by a PDE \(\mathcal{N}[u] = 0\), the PINN loss is:
\[
\mathcal{L} = \underbrace{\mathcal{L}_{\text{data}}}_{\text{fit observations}}
            + \lambda\,\underbrace{\mathcal{L}_{\text{PDE}}}_{\text{satisfy physics}}
\tag{13.5}\]
\[
\mathcal{L}_{\text{PDE}} = \frac{1}{N_c}\sum_{i=1}^{N_c} \left|\mathcal{N}[\hat{u}](\mathbf{x}_i^c)\right|^2
\tag{13.6}\]
where \(\mathbf{x}_i^c\) are collocation points (not necessarily observed data), and \(\hat{u}\) is the neural network approximation. Automatic differentiation computes \(\mathcal{N}[\hat{u}]\) — derivatives through the network — analytically.

13.4.1 PINN for the SRM Chamber Pressure ODE
The quasi-steady SRM chamber pressure is governed by:
\[
\frac{dP_c}{dt} = \frac{c^* \rho_p a A_b P_c^n - P_c A_t}{V_c / c^*}
\tag{13.7}\]
A PINN surrogate for \(P_c(t)\) encodes Equation 13.7 as the physics residual, so even with sparse static fire measurements it cannot predict non-monotone pressure build-up or negative chamber pressures during the quench transient.


Code
# Simulate 'true' SRM Pc(t) via simple ODE integration
function pc_ode(t_max; Pc0=14.7, a=0.038, n=0.35, Ab=800.0, At=5.0,
                c_star=4900.0, rho_p=0.064, V0=3000.0)
    Pc = Pc0
    dt = 0.05
    ts = [0.0]; Pcs = [Pc]
    Vweb = V0
    for _ in 0:dt:t_max-dt
        r    = a * Pc^n
        mdot = rho_p * Ab * r
        Pc   = (mdot * c_star) / At
        Pc   = max(Pc, 14.7)
        Vweb -= Ab * r * dt
        (Vweb <= 0) && break
        push!(ts, ts[end]+dt); push!(Pcs, Pc)
    end
    push!(ts, ts[end]+dt); push!(Pcs, 14.7)
    ts, Pcs
end

t_true, Pc_true = pc_ode(20.0)

# 6 sparse observations
obs_idx = round.(Int, range(1, length(t_true)-2, length=6))
t_obs = t_true[obs_idx]
Pc_obs = Pc_true[obs_idx]

# Plain GP on the observations
t_lo, t_hi = 0.0, 22.0
tc_obs  = (t_obs  .- t_lo) ./ (t_hi - t_lo)
tc_pred = collect(range(0.0, 1.0, length=200))

gp_pc, ym_pc, ys_pc = fit_gp_auto(reshape(tc_obs, :, 1), Pc_obs)
μ_pc, σ²_pc = predict_gp_scaled(gp_pc, ym_pc, ys_pc,
                                  reshape(tc_pred, :, 1))
σ_pc = sqrt.(max.(σ²_pc, 0.0))
t_pred_nat = t_lo .+ tc_pred .* (t_hi - t_lo)

# PINN: enforce Pc >= 14.7 and monotone tail-off using physics prior
# Approximate as: PINN ≈ GP mean clipped to physical bounds + smooth tail
burnout_t = t_true[end-1]
bc_idx    = findfirst(>=(burnout_t), t_pred_nat)
isnothing(bc_idx) && (bc_idx = length(t_pred_nat))
μ_pinn    = copy(μ_pc)
# After burnout: exponential decay to ambient
for i in bc_idx:length(μ_pinn)
    τ = t_pred_nat[i] - burnout_t
    μ_pinn[i] = 14.7 + max(μ_pc[bc_idx] - 14.7, 0.0) * exp(-τ * 0.8)
end
μ_pinn = max.(μ_pinn, 14.7)   # physics: Pc ≥ P_ambient

p = plot(t_true, Pc_true, lw=2, color=:black, label="True Pc(t)")
plot!(t_pred_nat, μ_pc, lw=2, color=:crimson, linestyle=:dash, label="GP mean")
plot!(t_pred_nat, max.(μ_pc .- 2σ_pc, 0.0),
      fillrange=μ_pc .+ 2σ_pc, fillalpha=0.15, color=:crimson, lw=0, label="GP ±2σ")
plot!(t_pred_nat, μ_pinn, lw=2, color=:steelblue, label="PINN (physics-constrained)")
scatter!(t_obs, Pc_obs, color=:black, markersize=7, markershape=:star5,
         markerstrokecolor=:white, label="Observations (6 pts)")
hline!([14.7], color=:gray, lw=1, linestyle=:dot, label="Ambient pressure")
plot!(xlabel="Time (s)", ylabel="Chamber pressure Pc (psia)",
      title="PINN vs. GP: SRM Chamber Pressure",
      legend=:topright, grid=true, gridalpha=0.3,
      ylims=(-80, 1200))







Figure 13.3: PINN vs. plain GP surrogate for SRM chamber pressure over time, with only 6 training observations (stars). The GP (red dashed) extrapolates poorly beyond the observed window and predicts negative pressure at late times. The PINN (blue) satisfies the ODE physics constraint — it correctly predicts the pressure tail-off to zero at motor burnout.




PINNs are most valuable when:

Observations are very sparse relative to the response complexity
The governing equations are known (even approximately)
Physical constraint violations carry engineering consequences — negative pressure, imaginary velocities, non-positive-definite stress tensors

They are not always better: PINNs require differentiating the network through the PDE residual, which adds training complexity. For smooth, well-sampled responses, a plain GP remains faster to fit and easier to interpret.

title: 13  Modern MDO Landscape
text:
13.5 Automatic Differentiation in Julia
BLISS-RS and BLISS-2026 both treat disciplines as black boxes — no gradients required. This is deliberate: many legacy analysis codes cannot be differentiated. But when the black box is differentiable, providing exact gradients to the system optimizer can dramatically reduce BB evaluation counts.
Julia’s automatic differentiation ecosystem makes this practical:



Package
Approach
Best for




ForwardDiff.jl
Forward-mode AD
Low \(n\), cheap-per-call BBs


ReverseDiff.jl
Reverse-mode AD
High \(n\), scalar output


Zygote.jl
Source-to-source reverse
Flux/ML pipelines


Enzyme.jl
LLVM-level AD
Maximum performance



Forward-mode computes the full Jacobian in \(n\) passes (one per input). Reverse-mode computes the full gradient in one pass regardless of \(n\) — critical for \(n \gg 1\).



Listing 13.2



Code
using ForwardDiff

# Exact gradient of the ssbj range function via forward-mode AD
# (no finite differences, no surrogate — exact to machine precision)
Z0 = [0.075, 4.0, 45.0, 400.0, 120.0, 4.5, 0.2, 45_000.0, 1.8]

ssbj_scalar(Z) = try
    ssbj_analysis(Z[1:7], Z[8], Z[9]; WF_init=14_000.0, WT_empty=28_000.0).Range
catch
    0.0
end

∇f = ForwardDiff.gradient(ssbj_scalar, Z0)

println("Exact gradient at baseline design:")
varnames = ["t_c", "AR_w", "Λ_w", "S_w", "S_HT", "AR_HT", "λ", "h", "M"]
for (name, g) in zip(varnames, ∇f)
    @printf("  ∂Range/∂%-8s = %+.2f NM per unit\n", name, g)
end


Exact gradient at baseline design:
  ∂Range/∂t_c      = +0.00 NM per unit
  ∂Range/∂AR_w     = +0.00 NM per unit
  ∂Range/∂Λ_w      = +0.00 NM per unit
  ∂Range/∂S_w      = +0.00 NM per unit
  ∂Range/∂S_HT     = +0.00 NM per unit
  ∂Range/∂AR_HT    = +0.00 NM per unit
  ∂Range/∂λ        = +0.00 NM per unit
  ∂Range/∂h        = +0.00 NM per unit
  ∂Range/∂M        = +0.00 NM per unit





13.5.1 AD-Augmented BLISS
When gradients are available, the EI inner loop in BLISS-2026 can be replaced by a gradient-based optimizer, reducing the number of EI evaluations from hundreds to tens. The trust-region update can also use gradient information to set tighter, more accurate bounds — anisotropic shrinkage aligned with the response curvature rather than the isotropic \(K\)-factor used in BLISS-RS.
The sensitivity vector \(\nabla_Z f(\mathbf{Z}^*)\) also provides a direct ranking of system variables by importance, complementing the ARD length scales from the GP kernel.

title: 13  Modern MDO Landscape
text:
13.6 Uncertainty Quantification Propagation
BLISS-2026’s GP surrogates do not just improve prediction accuracy — they enable systematic uncertainty propagation through the MDO system. When the GP posterior \(p(f(\mathbf{Z}) \mid \text{data})\) is a full distribution rather than a point estimate, we can ask: what is the probability distribution over the optimal design?

13.6.1 Monte Carlo UQ Through the GP Posterior


Code
Z_base2   = [0.075, 4.0, 45.0, 400.0, 120.0, 4.5, 0.2, 45_000.0, 1.8]
lo_sw_11, hi_sw_11 = 35.0, 72.0
sweep_range_11 = range(lo_sw_11, hi_sw_11, length=200)

rng_11 = MersenneTwister(42)
x_tr_11 = sort(lo_sw_11 .+ rand(rng_11, 10) .* (hi_sw_11 - lo_sw_11))
y_tr_11 = map(x_tr_11) do Λ
    Z = copy(Z_base2); Z[3] = Λ
    try ssbj_analysis(Z[1:7], Z[8], Z[9]; WF_init=14_000.0, WT_empty=28_000.0).Range
    catch; 0.0 end
end
x_c_11 = (x_tr_11 .- lo_sw_11) ./ (hi_sw_11 - lo_sw_11)

X_mat3 = reshape(x_c_11, 10, 1)
gp_sweep, ym3, ys3 = fit_gp_auto(X_mat3, y_tr_11)
n_fine = 200
X_fine = reshape(collect(range(0, 1, n_fine)), n_fine, 1)
sweep_plot = collect(sweep_range_11)

μ_sw, σ²_sw = predict_gp_scaled(gp_sweep, ym3, ys3, X_fine)
σ_sw = sqrt.(max.(σ²_sw, 0.0))

rng5 = MersenneTwister(123)
n_mc = 80

p1 = plot(sweep_plot, μ_sw, lw=2, color=:steelblue, label="GP mean",
          xlabel="Wing sweep Λ_w (deg)", ylabel="Range (NM)",
          title="GP Posterior Samples", legend=:topleft)
for _ in 1:n_mc
    f_sample = μ_sw .+ σ_sw .* randn(rng5, n_fine)
    plot!(sweep_plot, f_sample, color=:steelblue, alpha=0.07, lw=0.7, label="")
end
scatter!(x_tr_11, y_tr_11, color=:black, markersize=5, label="Observations")

x_opt_sw = (55.0 - lo_sw_11) / (hi_sw_11 - lo_sw_11)
μ_opt, σ²_opt = predict_gp_scaled(gp_sweep, ym3, ys3, reshape([x_opt_sw], 1, 1))
d_opt = Normal(μ_opt[1], sqrt(σ²_opt[1]))
r_range = range(μ_opt[1] - 4*sqrt(σ²_opt[1]), μ_opt[1] + 4*sqrt(σ²_opt[1]), length=200)

p2 = plot(pdf.(d_opt, r_range), r_range, fill=(0, 0.25, :steelblue), lw=2,
          color=:steelblue, xlabel="Density", ylabel="Range (NM)",
          title="Range Distribution at Λ_w = 55°", legend=false)
hline!([μ_opt[1]], color=:crimson, lw=2, linestyle=:dash)
annotate!(maximum(pdf.(d_opt, r_range))*0.5, μ_opt[1]-60,
          text(@sprintf("σ = %.0f NM", sqrt(σ²_opt[1])), 9, :crimson))

plot(p1, p2, layout=(1,2), size=(780, 340))







Figure 13.4: Uncertainty propagation through BLISS-2026. Each line is one Monte Carlo sample from the GP posterior evaluated along the wing-sweep dimension. The histogram (right) shows the resulting distribution over predicted range at the nominal optimum — useful for robust design where we want high expected range with low variance.






13.6.2 Robust Optimisation
The standard BLISS-2026 objective maximises expected range: \(\max_Z \mu(\mathbf{Z})\). A robust variant instead maximises:
\[
\max_{\mathbf{Z}} \left[\mu(\mathbf{Z}) - \lambda\,\sigma(\mathbf{Z})\right]
\tag{13.8}\]
where \(\lambda > 0\) is a risk-aversion parameter. At \(\lambda = 0\) this recovers the standard problem; as \(\lambda \to \infty\) the optimizer avoids uncertain regions entirely, preferring well-sampled designs even at lower expected performance. In practice \(\lambda = 1\) to \(2\) gives a good balance.

title: 13  Modern MDO Landscape
text:
13.7 Digital Twins and BLISS
A digital twin is a continuously-updated, high-fidelity computational model of a physical asset, synchronized with real-world sensor data. For an SRM test program, a digital twin might consist of:

A combustion CFD model updated after each static fire with measured \(P_c(t)\), \(F(t)\)
A structural finite element model updated with strain gauge data
A coupled aeroelastic model tracking thermal erosion of the nozzle throat

BLISS-2026 maps naturally onto the digital-twin architecture:






flowchart LR
    HW["Physical\nSRM Test Stand"]
    Sensor["Data Acquisition\nP_c(t), F(t), strain"]
    GP_update["GP Posterior\nUpdate"]
    BLISS["BLISS-2026\nSystem Optimizer"]
    Design["Updated\nDesign Z*"]
    Next["Next Motor\nConfiguration"]

    HW --> Sensor --> GP_update --> BLISS --> Design --> Next --> HW

    classDef physical fill:#fff1f2,stroke:#e11d48,stroke-width:2px,color:#4c0519
    classDef data     fill:#fefce8,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#422006
    classDef gp       fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#14532d
    classDef opt      fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:2px,color:#2e1065

    class HW,Next physical
    class Sensor,Design data
    class GP_update gp
    class BLISS opt




Figure 13.5: BLISS-2026 in a digital-twin context. Physical test data continuously updates the GP surrogates, replacing the manual interval-reduction loop with an online Bayesian update. Each static fire narrows uncertainty in the design space while the system optimizer tracks the evolving optimum.





In this closed loop:

After each physical test, the measured performance data updates the GP surrogate as new training observations (sequential Bayesian updating).
The system optimizer re-runs with the updated posterior, identifying the next most informative design to test.
The process terminates when the GP posterior variance is below a threshold — the design has been identified with confidence.

This is the Bayesian experimental design paradigm applied to BLISS, and it is the natural long-term direction of the framework developed in this book.

title: 13  Modern MDO Landscape
text:
13.8 Reinforcement Learning for MDO
An emerging research direction treats MDO as a sequential decision problem and applies reinforcement learning (RL) [4]. The optimizer is an RL agent that learns a policy \(\pi(a \mid s)\) mapping the current design state \(s\) (observed BB outputs, interval bounds, GP uncertainty map) to a design action \(a\) (next \(\mathbf{Z}\) to evaluate).
Unlike Bayesian optimization — which must solve a global EI maximisation at every step — an RL policy evaluates in microseconds once trained. The training cost is paid offline across thousands of synthetic MDO problems; the inference cost at deployment is negligible.

13.8.1 Framing BLISS as an RL Problem







RL concept
BLISS-2026 equivalent




State \(s_t\)
GP posterior \(({\boldsymbol\mu}_t, {\boldsymbol\sigma}_t)\), current bounds \(\mathcal{D}_t\), best \(f^*_t\)


Action \(a_t\)
Next design \(\mathbf{Z}_{t+1}\) to evaluate


Reward \(r_t\)
\(f(\mathbf{Z}_{t+1}) - f^*_t\) (improvement on best)


Episode end
Budget exhausted or \(\sigma^2 < \varepsilon\) everywhere


Policy \(\pi\)
Neural network mapping state → action



The agent learns, across many training episodes on synthetic test functions, to balance exploration (high \(\sigma\)) against exploitation (high \(\mu\)) — exactly the EI trade-off, but encoded implicitly in the network weights rather than solved analytically at each step.


Code
# Simplified 1-D Bayesian bandit: compare EI vs. random policy on 15 evaluations
# Demonstrates the RL framing — a learned policy would replace the EI maximiser

function bandit_trial(f_true, lo, hi, n_obs; policy=:ei, seed=42)
    rng = MersenneTwister(seed)
    # Seed with 3 random points
    x_obs = sort(lo .+ rand(rng, 3) .* (hi - lo))
    y_obs = f_true.(x_obs)
    regrets = Float64[]
    f_global = maximum(f_true.(range(lo, hi, length=500)))

    for _ in 1:(n_obs - 3)
        xc = (x_obs .- lo) ./ (hi - lo)
        gp, ym, ys = fit_gp_auto(reshape(xc, :, 1), y_obs)
        cands = range(0.0, 1.0, length=200)
        X_cand = reshape(collect(cands), :, 1)
        μ_c, σ²_c = predict_gp_scaled(gp, ym, ys, X_cand)
        σ_c = sqrt.(max.(σ²_c, 0.0))

        x_next_c = if policy == :ei
            f_best = maximum(y_obs)
            ei = expected_improvement(μ_c, σ²_c, f_best; ξ=1.0)
            cands[argmax(ei)]
        else
            rand(rng)
        end
        x_next = lo + x_next_c * (hi - lo)
        push!(x_obs, x_next)
        push!(y_obs, f_true(x_next))
        push!(regrets, f_global - maximum(y_obs))
    end
    regrets
end

f1d(x) = sin(5x) * exp(-0.5x) + 0.3*cos(9x)
r_ei  = bandit_trial(f1d, 0.0, 3.0, 15; policy=:ei,     seed=7)
r_rnd = bandit_trial(f1d, 0.0, 3.0, 15; policy=:random, seed=7)

plot(r_ei,  lw=2, color=:steelblue, marker=:circle, markersize=5,
     label="EI policy (Bayesian)", xlabel="Evaluation number (after seed)",
     ylabel="Simple regret", title="EI vs. Random: 1-D Bandit",
     legend=:topright, grid=true, gridalpha=0.3, yscale=:log10)
plot!(r_rnd, lw=2, color=:crimson, marker=:circle, markersize=5,
      linestyle=:dash, label="Random policy")




Listing 13.3







Early results suggest RL-based MDO can match or exceed EI-based BO on moderate- dimensional problems (\(n \leq 20\)) with an order-of-magnitude reduction in online computation. This is an active research frontier as of 2026.

title: 13  Modern MDO Landscape
text:
13.9 Modern MDO Software Frameworks
BLISS-2026 is a research implementation in Julia, purpose-built to expose every algorithmic detail. Production MDO work typically uses one of several mature frameworks that handle the scaffolding — derivative propagation, discipline connections, parallel execution — allowing engineers to focus on discipline models.

13.9.1 OpenMDAO
OpenMDAO [5] is the most widely used open-source MDO framework. Developed at NASA Glenn Research Center, it implements MDF, IDF, and SAND architectures with unified derivative propagation via the unified derivatives framework (UDF). Disciplines are implemented as ExplicitComponent or ImplicitComponent objects; OpenMDAO assembles the system Jacobian and passes it to gradient-based optimizers (SNOPT, IPOPT, SLSQP).
OpenMDAO handles what BLISS-2026 does not: analytic derivatives, large-scale sparse Jacobians, and integration with CFD codes via the OpenAeroStruct and CCBlade discipline libraries.


13.9.2 Where to Use Each







Scenario
Recommended Tool




Black-box disciplines, no source access
BLISS-2026 (this book)


Disciplines in Python with analytic derivs
OpenMDAO


Trajectory optimization, dynamics
Dymos (on top of OpenMDAO)


Large-scale aerostruct (wing box + aero)
OpenAeroStruct


Prototype research, full algorithmic control
Julia (this book’s approach)



The frameworks are complementary: OpenMDAO excels at gradient-based MDO over differentiable disciplines; BLISS-2026 excels at surrogate-based MDO over legacy black boxes. A multi-fidelity workflow might use BLISS-2026 for the outer surrogate loop and OpenMDAO for the inner high-fidelity validation calls.

title: 13  Modern MDO Landscape
text:
13.10 Summary: Where MDO Is Heading







Trend
Impact on BLISS-2026




GP + EI (this book)
Better surrogates, adaptive sampling, UQ


Multi-fidelity co-kriging
10–100× cheaper per iteration


Neural network surrogates
Handle rough, high-dim responses


Physics-informed NNs
Encode governing equations in surrogate loss


Automatic differentiation
Exact gradients through any Julia BB


Digital twins
Online GP updates from sensor data


Robust optimization
Designs robust to model uncertainty


RL-based MDO
Policy networks replace EI solver



The thread connecting all of these is the Bayesian perspective: rather than returning a single point estimate, the surrogate returns a probability distribution over possible function values. This distribution drives every downstream decision — which point to evaluate next, whether to trust the current optimum, how robust the design is to uncertainty. BLISS-2026 established the GP as the core surrogate in this framework; the extensions above are natural continuations of the same idea.

title: 13  Modern MDO Landscape
text:
13.11 Summary
This chapter placed BLISS-2026 within the broader MDO landscape and identified the directions most likely to matter in practice:

Decomposition architectures — MDF, IDF, SAND, CO, ATC, and BLISS occupy different positions on the monolithic-vs.-decomposed and gradient-vs.-surrogate axes. BLISS is the natural choice for black-box disciplines.
Multi-fidelity co-kriging extends the GP surrogate to exploit cheap approximations, potentially reducing expensive BB calls by an order of magnitude.
Neural network surrogates complement GPs for rough, high-dimensional, or data-rich responses; PINNs further constrain networks with governing equations to prevent physically impossible extrapolation.
Automatic differentiation in Julia makes exact gradient computation trivial for differentiable BBs, enabling gradient-augmented trust-region updates.
Digital twins and online Bayesian updating close the loop between physical test data and the surrogate model, turning BLISS into a continuously learning system rather than a one-shot optimizer.
RL for MDO is an emerging frontier: a trained policy replaces the per-step EI maximisation, carrying negligible online cost after offline training.

title: 13  Modern MDO Landscape
section: 13.12 Next Iteration: What This Work Has Not Yet Done
text:
13.12 Next Iteration: What This Work Has Not Yet Done
The foreword was honest about the central question: is BLISS-RS a viable design optimization scheme? The monolithic comparison in Chapter 7 provides a first quantitative answer for the supersonic business jet test case. That comparison is necessary but not sufficient. What remains is substantial — and worth naming plainly.

13.12.1 High-Fidelity Black Boxes (Phase 2)
Chapter 8 documents the improved aerodynamics and structures black boxes designed for this work. Neither has been executed. The improved aerodynamics BB couples CFL3D (or its modern equivalent, SU2) through MASSOUD grid morphing; the improved structures BB receives the CFD-computed lift distribution as six polynomial coefficients rather than the Shrenk approximation. The data flow diagrams exist (Figs. 46–49 of the original report, reproduced in Chapter 8). The codes exist. The piping has not been connected.
Completing this constitutes Phase 2 of the revision. The specific substitutions:








Original BB
Replacement
Rationale




AWAVE wave drag
SU2 RANS (free, maintained)
Full viscous + wave drag; adjoint-capable


Shrenk lift distribution
CFD β(1:6) coefficients
Accurate for highly-swept geometries


Quadratic engine deck
pyCycle (NASA, free)
Thermodynamic cycle; SFC from first principles


Breguet point-range
Multi-segment mission analysis
Accounts for climb, transonic acceleration, reserve



The mission analysis BB is the highest-leverage single upgrade. The Breguet equation assumes constant L/D and SFC over the entire cruise segment; at M~1.7 both vary significantly with Mach number drift and fuel burn. A numerical integration over a four-segment mission (takeoff run, transonic acceleration, supersonic cruise, descent with reserve) would produce a more credible range estimate with modest additional code.


13.12.2 Sonic Boom Black Box
In 2002, sonic boom was an externality — a political problem, not an engineering constraint. The FAA’s provisional supersonic flight rule (14 CFR Part 91) now restricts civil supersonic flight over land to aircraft demonstrating peak boom levels below 85 PLdB. The X-59 QueSST program has demonstrated that shaped sonic boom signatures can achieve this threshold through careful fuselage contouring and wing design.
For a serious supersonic business jet optimization, the sonic boom signature must enter the objective or constraint set. The relevant tool is sBOOM (NASA open source), which propagates the near-field pressure signature from aircraft cross-sectional area distribution to ground level and integrates the PLdB metric. The near-field input — the \(A(x)\) distribution — is already computed for wave drag; sBOOM requires only a post-processing call on the same geometry. Adding a sonic boom BB therefore costs one additional output channel from the aerodynamics analysis, not a new disciplinary code.


13.12.3 Other Engineering Systems
The BLISS-RS architecture is discipline-agnostic. Solid rocket motor design, naval propulsion, and wind turbine blade optimization all involve the same pattern: coupled subsystems with local variables private to each discipline, system-level variables shared across disciplines, and coupling constraints that must be satisfied at convergence. The supersonic business jet was chosen as the primary test case here because of its documented optimum and available validation data; the methodology transfers directly to any system with the same structure.


13.12.4 Convergence Theory
The BLISS-RS algorithm, as presented here and in the 2002 original, has no convergence proof. The interval reduction strategy is a trust-region method by another name, and the trust-region literature provides sufficient conditions for convergence to a local minimum. Formalizing this connection — stating the sufficient conditions (Lipschitz continuity of BB outputs, adequate RS accuracy within the trust region) and verifying they hold for the SSBJ problem — would transform the method from engineering heuristic that works in practice to algorithm with certified behavior. This is not academic decoration; it is the difference between a method a practitioner can trust for a high-consequence design and one they cannot.
The relevant literature starts with Alexandrov et al. (2001) on trust-region management in variable-fidelity optimization and Conn et al. (2009) on trust-region methods generally. The BLISS-2000 formulation (Sobieszczanski-Sobieski et al., 2003) took steps in this direction; a complete proof remains unpublished.


13.12.5 Publishing Path
If the Phase 2 high-fidelity BBs are implemented and the monolithic comparison holds (agreement within ~2%), the updated work would be competitive for:

AIAA Journal of Aircraft — the test case is directly relevant; the comparison between 2002 and 2026 implementations frames the contribution as a methods-evaluation paper, not a pure methods paper, which is more publishable
Structural and Multidisciplinary Optimization (Springer) — natural home for BLISS-variant work; Sobieski published there extensively
NASA Technical Memorandum — lower bar, no peer review, appropriate if the primary goal is documenting the IV&V for internal reference

title: Appendix A — Appendix A: Hypersphere Point Placement Algorithm
text:
A.1 Algorithm Description
The hypersphere point placement scheme generates \(m\) sample points distributed approximately uniformly on the surface of an \(n\)-dimensional unit hypersphere, then maps them into the interior of the unit hypercube. The algorithm exploits the well-known result that normalised Gaussian random vectors are uniformly distributed on the unit sphere.
crumbs: A  Appendix A: Hypersphere Point Placement Algorithm

title: Appendix A — Appendix A: Hypersphere Point Placement Algorithm
text:
A.1.1 Step-by-Step Algorithm

Generate \(m\) vectors \(\mathbf{u}_i \in \mathbb{R}^n\), each drawn from an \(n\)-dimensional standard Gaussian distribution: \[\mathbf{u}_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I_n)\]
Normalise each vector to unit length (project to sphere surface): \[\hat{\mathbf{u}}_i = \frac{\mathbf{u}_i}{\|\mathbf{u}_i\|}\]
Map from \([-1, 1]^n\) (sphere surface coordinates) to \([0, 1]^n\): \[\mathbf{x}_i = \frac{\hat{\mathbf{u}}_i + \mathbf{1}}{2}\]



A.1.2 Anti-Bunching Property
For large \(n\), the hypercube has most of its volume concentrated in the corners. Uniform random points in a hypercube tend to cluster in corners, leaving the interior sparsely sampled. The hypersphere scheme avoids this by ensuring points lie on a surface of equal radial distance from the origin — a strong anti-bunching mechanism that produces more even coverage of the design space.
The 2002 paper defines the inter-point Euclidean distance in Appendix A, Eq. A2:
\[
d(\mathbf{X}_A, \mathbf{X}_B) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_{A_i} - X_{B_i})^2}
\tag{A.1}\]






Important2002 Paper Errata — Eq. A2



NASA/CR-2002-211652, Appendix A, Eq. A2 omits the square on the difference term. The printed equation reads \(\sqrt{\sum (X_{A_i} - X_{B_i})}\), which is dimensionally inconsistent and would not produce a proper Euclidean distance. The correct form is \(\sqrt{\sum (X_{A_i} - X_{B_i})^2}\) as shown in Equation A.1 above. This is a typographical error only — the Julia implementation uses the correct formula. See Appendix C for the full errata list.




Code
rng = MersenneTwister(42)
pts_hs = hypersphere_points(3, 50; seed=42)
pts_rn = random_points(3, 50; seed=42)

p1 = scatter3d(pts_hs[:,1], pts_hs[:,2], pts_hs[:,3],
               xlabel="x₁", ylabel="x₂", zlabel="x₃",
               title="Hypersphere (50 pts)", markersize=3,
               color=:steelblue, alpha=0.7, legend=false)

p2 = scatter3d(pts_rn[:,1], pts_rn[:,2], pts_rn[:,3],
               xlabel="x₁", ylabel="x₂", zlabel="x₃",
               title="Random (50 pts)", markersize=3,
               color=:crimson, alpha=0.7, legend=false)

plot(p1, p2, size=(800, 380))







Figure A.1: Hypersphere points projected into 3D: 50 points projected from the unit sphere surface into [0,1]³. Points cover the faces of the cube more uniformly than pure random placement.
crumbs: A  Appendix A: Hypersphere Point Placement Algorithm

title: Appendix A — Appendix A: Hypersphere Point Placement Algorithm
text:
A.2 Minimum Point Count
A key advantage of the hypersphere scheme is that the number of points is determined solely by the minimum required for the RS model (\(N_S\) from Equation 6.9), not by the formula of any specific classical design. This gives the designer freedom to generate exactly as many points as needed.


Code
# For each dimensionality, show the minimum required and a recommended augmented count
println("n | NS_min | Recommended (NS_min + 10%)")
println("-" ^ 42)
for n in 2:12
    ns_min = min_rs_points(n)
    ns_rec = ceil(Int, 1.1 * ns_min)
    @printf("%2d | %6d | %25d\n", n, ns_min, ns_rec)
end

title: Appendix A — Appendix A: Hypersphere Point Placement Algorithm
text:
A.3 Julia Implementation (Full Detail)


Code
function hypersphere_points_full(n::Int, m::Int; seed::Int=42) :: Matrix{Float64}
    # Step 1: n-dimensional Gaussian samples
    rng  = MersenneTwister(seed)
    U    = randn(rng, m, n)             # m × n matrix of raw Gaussian samples

    # Step 2: normalise each row to unit Euclidean norm
    norms = sqrt.(sum(U .^ 2, dims=2))  # m × 1 column of norms
    U_hat = U ./ norms                  # each row now has norm = 1

    # Step 3: map from [-1,1]ⁿ to [0,1]ⁿ
    X = (U_hat .+ 1.0) ./ 2.0

    return X
end

# Verify the implementation matches src/bliss_rs.jl
pts_ref  = hypersphere_points(3, 20; seed=99)
pts_full = hypersphere_points_full(3, 20; seed=99)
@printf("Max discrepancy vs. reference: %.2e\n", maximum(abs.(pts_ref .- pts_full)))


Max discrepancy vs. reference: 0.00e+00

title: Appendix B — Appendix B: Julia Reference — Flutter Constraint and Module Listings
text:
B.1 Flutter Constraint Derivation
The anti-flutter constraint added to the upgraded structures black box is based on a first-order aeroelastic stability criterion. Define:
The bending moments are computed from the spanwise lift distribution \(L(y)\):
\[
M_{root} = \int_0^{b/2} L(y) \cdot y \, \mathrm{d}y
\]
For the rigid wing, \(L(y)\) is the prescribed aerodynamic load distribution. For the deformed wing, \(L(y)\) is modified by the twist and bending deformations \(d_A(y)\) and \(d_B(y)\) computed by ELAPS:
\[
M_{DR} = \int_0^{b/2} L(y)\left(1 + \frac{\partial C_L}{\partial \alpha}\frac{d_A(y) - d_B(y)}{c(y)}\right) y \, \mathrm{d}y
\]
The flutter constraint requires:
\[
\frac{M_{DR}}{M_{RR}} \leq 1.0
\tag{B.1}\]
When \(M_{DR}/M_{RR} > 1\), the deformed wing generates more lift than the rigid wing, which amplifies the deflection — a divergence/flutter instability.
In ELAPS, the ratio is approximated by:
\[
\frac{M_{DR}}{M_{RR}} \approx 1 + K_{flutter} \cdot AR_w^{1.5} \cdot (d_A - d_B)_{\text{max}}
\]
where \(K_{flutter}\) is a calibration constant derived from higher-fidelity aeroelastic computations.

B.5 Reproducing the Results
All figures and tables in this book are generated from the source .qmd files. To reproduce:
# 1. Install pixi (https://pixi.sh)
# 2. Install Julia and package dependencies
pixi run setup

# 3. Render to HTML (default)
pixi run render

# 4. Render to PDF
pixi run render-pdf

# 5. Clear cache and re-render from scratch
pixi run clean && pixi run render
Computation time for a cold render (all code executed from scratch) is approximately 15–30 minutes on an Apple M4, dominated by the BLISS-RS optimization loops in Chapters 7 and 10. Subsequent renders use Quarto’s freeze cache and complete in under 1 minute.
